pages.title: Grease Buildup
pages.summary: Professional overview of kitchen grease buildup: formation, risks, and how operators approach removal without damaging finishes.

image.primaryAlt: Grease buildup on kitchen cabinet near stove
queries: grease buildup kitchen cabinets stove area
oily residue kitchen surfaces professional cleaning
cooking grease film on cabinets close up

sections.what_this_is: Grease buildup is the accumulation of cooking oils, aerosolized fats, and hand-transfer residue on kitchen surfaces—especially near the range, on cabinets, backsplashes, hoods, and adjacent walls.
sections.why_it_happens: Heat and cooking vapor deposit hydrophobic films that bind to paint, wood sealers, metal, and tile. Over time layers polymerize and become harder to remove with neutral cleaners alone.
sections.what_people_do_wrong: Operators often under-clean with mild sprays, skip dwell time, or attack polymerized film with abrasives that damage finishes.
sections.professional_method: Identify finish and soil severity, use a degreaser matched to the material, allow controlled dwell, agitate with non-scratch tools, rinse or wipe residue, and dry to limit streaking.
sections.data_and_benchmarks: Severity scales with cooking frequency, ventilation quality, and maintenance gaps. Light films often release in one pass; layered or baked-on residue typically needs repeat treatment.
sections.professional_insights: Grease often reads as dust until a side-angle light check reveals an oily sheen. Chemistry and dwell usually outperform force.
sections.when_to_call_a_professional: Call a professional when finishes are delicate, residue spans ceilings or large wall areas, or DIY attempts have dulled or damaged surfaces.

pages.title: Grease on Kitchen Cabinets
pages.summary: Surface-specific guidance for grease on painted, laminate, and sealed wood kitchen cabinets near cooking zones.

image.primaryAlt: Grease marks on white kitchen cabinet doors
queries: grease stains kitchen cabinet doors cleaning
oily cabinet fronts near stove
kitchen cabinet degreasing before painting prep

sections.what_this_is: Cabinet grease is localized oily film or streaking on door faces, frames, and hardware zones most exposed to cooking plumes and hand contact.
sections.why_it_happens: Vertical surfaces near the range receive repeated vapor and splatter. Handles and edges collect transfer oils from daily use.
sections.what_people_do_wrong: Spraying excess product that seeps into seams, using harsh pads on painted or thermofoil fronts, or wiping once without rotating clean towel faces.
sections.professional_method: Protect floors, work top-to-bottom on small sections, use finish-safe degreaser loading on the towel, fold-and-rotate, and dry-polish to remove residue.
sections.data_and_benchmarks: Flat paint and some thermofoil finishes are less forgiving than sealed wood or laminate; test spots reduce risk.
sections.professional_insights: The tell is often a tacky or rainbows-under-light sheen rather than loose dust.
sections.when_to_call_a_professional: Call when coatings are unknown, when gloss has dulled from prior attempts, or when grease has migrated to crown or ceiling lines.

pages.title: Grease on Stainless Steel
pages.summary: How grease behaves on brushed stainless appliances and how to remove it without streaking or grain damage.

image.primaryAlt: Fingerprints and grease on stainless refrigerator
queries: stainless steel appliance grease fingerprints cleaning
brushed stainless streak free degreasing
range hood stainless steel grease removal

sections.what_this_is: Stainless steel in kitchens shows grease as smudges, directional streaks, and dull patches that follow the grain of the finish.
sections.why_it_happens: Cooking aerosols and hand oils adhere strongly; brushed grain makes uneven residue highly visible under side lighting.
sections.what_people_do_wrong: Wiping against the grain, over-wetting electronics-adjacent panels, or leaving cleaner residue that reads as new streaks.
sections.professional_method: Dry dust first, apply light degreaser or stainless-appropriate cleaner, wipe with the grain in long passes, then dry-buff with clean microfiber.
sections.data_and_benchmarks: Residue control matters more than product brand; excess liquid is a common cause of poor appearance.
sections.professional_insights: Treat hoods and vertical fridge panels as finish-sensitive; control product volume strictly.
sections.when_to_call_a_professional: Call when coatings are specialty black stainless or when pitting or discoloration suggests damage, not soil.

pages.title: Grease on Backsplashes
pages.summary: Removing cooking grease from tile, stone, and composite backsplashes while protecting grout and sealers.

image.primaryAlt: Grease splatter on kitchen tile backsplash
queries: greasy tile backsplash behind stove
kitchen backsplash degreasing grout safe
cooking splatter on subway tile cleaning

sections.what_this_is: Backsplash grease is splatter and vapor-deposited film on vertical surfaces behind cooktops, often concentrated in a low band behind burners.
sections.why_it_happens: Boiling, frying, and high-heat cooking propel fine oil droplets that adhere to grout pores and tile glaze irregularities.
sections.what_people_do_wrong: Using the wrong chemistry for natural stone, flooding grout lines with liquid, or scrubbing unsealed grout into discoloration.
sections.professional_method: Identify tile and stone type, dry-remove loose soil, apply compatible degreaser, dwell briefly, agitate with non-scratch tools, and remove suspended soil without dirty rinse pooling.
sections.data_and_benchmarks: Porous grout holds grease longer than glazed tile; targeted grout attention often determines overall appearance.
sections.professional_insights: Pattern matters: a horizontal band behind the cooktop usually confirms vapor deposition versus random splatter.
sections.when_to_call_a_professional: Call when stone type is uncertain, when grout is heavily stained, or when sealers may need reassessment after cleaning.

pages.title: Grease on Walls Near the Stove
pages.summary: Addressing grease on painted drywall and trim adjacent to ranges without burnishing or lifting paint.

image.primaryAlt: Discolored wall area next to kitchen stove
queries: grease on painted wall behind stove
kitchen wall yellowing near range cleaning
flat paint grease spot removal wall

sections.what_this_is: Wall-adjacent grease presents as yellowing, tacky patches, or dull smears on paint within the thermal and vapor plume of the stove.
sections.why_it_happens: Heat drives oils onto vertical paint films; flat and matte paints embed soil more visibly than glossier finishes.
sections.what_people_do_wrong: Scrubbing aggressively on delicate paint, saturating drywall, or using strong solvents that soften coatings.
sections.professional_method: Use minimal moisture, work in small arcs with lightly loaded towels, step up chemistry only as needed, and blot-dry quickly.
sections.data_and_benchmarks: Older flats often require gentler sequences; repeated light passes beat one heavy wet pass.
sections.professional_insights: If color lifts onto the towel, stop and reassess—this is coating failure risk, not a soil-only issue.
sections.when_to_call_a_professional: Call when paint is failing, when large areas are involved, or when touch-up and reprep are likely after soil removal.

pages.title: Polymerized Grease Residue
pages.summary: Recognizing heat-cured grease films that resist quick wipes and require controlled chemistry and dwell—not just more pressure.

image.primaryAlt: Baked on brown grease on range hood filter area
queries: baked on grease range hood sticky residue
polymerized oil residue stovetop edge
brown hardened grease kitchen cleaning professional

sections.what_this_is: Polymerized grease is a hardened or highly tacky film formed when cooking oils undergo heat-driven chemical change, common on hoods, stove edges, and nearby metal or enamel.
sections.why_it_happens: Repeated heat cycles crosslink residues into a layer that neutral surfactants struggle to emulsify quickly.
sections.what_people_do_wrong: Increasing scrub pressure instead of dwell, using abrasive pads on coated metals, or chipping at edges and damaging finishes.
sections.professional_method: Select a degreaser rated for the substrate, apply evenly, allow sufficient dwell, reapply as needed, agitate gently, and fully remove loosened residue.
sections.data_and_benchmarks: Thicker polymerized layers often need multiple controlled cycles rather than a single heavy application.
sections.professional_insights: If the surface feels smooth but visually cloudy, you may be seeing partially broken film—finish with clean dry passes.
sections.when_to_call_a_professional: Call when near electrical components, when coatings are unknown, or when damage or corrosion is visible under residue.

pages.title: Product Residue Buildup
pages.summary: Cleaner and polish buildup that dulls surfaces, causes streaking, and masquerades as permanent soil.

queries: cleaner residue streaks on countertop
product buildup dull quartz surface
too much floor cleaner residue haze

sections.what_this_is: Product residue buildup is accumulated surfactants, polymers, oils, or wax components left from over-application, incomplete rinsing, or incompatible stacking of products.
sections.why_it_happens: More product does not always mean cleaner; without removal and drying, films accumulate and attract dust.
sections.what_people_do_wrong: Layering multi-surface sprays, mopping with strong concentrate ratios, or buffing without verifying true soil removal.
sections.professional_method: Strip the assumption of grease or hard water first—test a small low-residue rinse sequence, use compatible chemistry to dissolve the film, and dry-finish thoroughly.
sections.data_and_benchmarks: Residue issues often spike after product changes or after deep cleans that skipped rinse steps.
sections.professional_insights: If soil returns quickly after a perfect-looking wipe, suspect film—not new dirt.
sections.when_to_call_a_professional: Call when stone or coated floors are involved, when manufacturer care guides conflict, or when etching could be mistaken for residue.
